title: Closing Bell: AI winners in plain sight at conference
full_text:
New money has found one of Australia's oldest stories, and it's all because of electrification and AI.
It's hard to believe there were hefty global investors out there not across BHP and its strategic pivot to copper, but apparently, the penny dropped. Generalist investors - not mining investors - have swamped its share register in the past two months, sending its shares racing above $50.
BHP chief financial officer Vandita Pant says she's seen it on the Big Australian's share register. Macquarie analyst Rob Stein, head of resources research, said he's got the same new names asking for his views on BHP's share price for the first time.
"They are looking upstream and saying 'where's the bottleneck?'," Pant says, and obviously decided some of it is in copper. BHP is already the world's biggest copper miner, has been talking up its 3 per cent to 4 per cent annual production growth for the coming decade and led the investors' horses to the water, so to speak.
That it is generalist investors - think the big funds in Boston and New York that invest across sectors and up and down capital structures - is significant. New interest, and dare we say hot interest, is always needed to take BHP's share price to a record high; AustralianSuper and BlackRock's Evy Hambro are not doing it on their own.
That it's already happened, and that BHP's share price is still going up and to the right, means we need to think about how Australia can win from the AI capital expenditure boom.
We don't have chip companies - the ones that are up 40 per cent-plus in barely a month - or the hyperscalers whose $US750 billion ($1 trillion) annual capex is ultimately paying for the data centre build out, but we do have all sorts of other companies scrambling to find their angle. BHP's copper pitch, which culminated in it putting 10-year production growth forecasts into the market, was a knockout punch.
Stock picking is storytelling, and just about every other big company is trying to find its AI story - it's on full display at the Macquarie Australia Conference this week.
Some are selling assets into the AI boom (Charter Hall), others are twisting their business models to service it (Stockland, HMC Capital) and others, such as BHP, AGL Energy, Origin Energy, Soul Patts and the gas plays, are diverting more attention to what they've already got.
Either way, it's a gold rush. Infratil-owned CDC Data Centres showed just how much of a gold rush it is; its new 555-megawatt contract for Australian data centres was more than twice as large as the previous largest (NextDC's 250 megawatts, big news when it was signed just a fortnight ago).
The first derivatives are real estate owners and data centre developers, the next are chipmakers and energy companies, then from there we get to miners, engineering groups, services and the tenants and funders, the big tech companies.
Every company's going to be an AI user, and nearly all of them told investors they already were - it came up at SGH Limited, Transurban Group, Westpac, Qantas, WiseTech Global and most others we saw.
BHP shows it takes a lot of hard work to reorient a big organisation towards a big trend such as AI and electrification, but the rewards are there once the penny drops with investors.
Here's what else caught our eye at the Macquarie conference on day two:
You are what you wear | JB Hi-Fi boss Nick Wells says he didn't imagine selling sunglasses five years ago, but here we are. Wearables such as glasses and rings are getting more space inside his JB Hi-Fi stores, and he says that trend's only going one way.
The big thing for him now is cost. Anything with memory or chips is costing more, thanks to the data-centre boom and AI squeeze. Chipmakers are run off their feet, and he thinks it could take another three to six months for them to get used to higher demand. Meanwhile, anything with memory in it - laptops for example - will get more expensive.
Wells says it's important for JB Hi-Fi to keep servicing customers at all price points. If a customer wants to spend $999 on a laptop, JB Hi-Fi has to give them one even if it has half the memory.
Cost control | Transurban CFO Henry Byrne did CFOs everywhere proud when he spent the opening five minutes - the start of the session where investors are not on their phones - talking about costs.
He says the toll road operator is set to record its third consecutive year with cost growth running below CPI growth, partly thanks to pretty steep job cuts, and made it clear he and chief executive Michelle Jablko have further to go.
"Is that cost growth below CPI official guidance," Macquarie analyst Ian Myles asked. Byrne said no, but he wanted investors to take it as a statement of intent.
Capital concern | The other big thing hanging over every session was capital costs. Interest rates are up, inflation is up and business is hunkering down for a world where capital costs more and return hurdles will be higher.
HMC Capital's David Di Pilla, who's chopped and changed the company strategy to retreat from specialist private equity funds and offshore data centres, had some clear thoughts on it: "The cost of capital is going up," he says. "Liquidity is tightening, and so it is a good time to be getting your balance sheet into really strong shape to be in a priority posture going into the next 12 months ... We think the world is very price- and capital-sensitive."
Di Pilla should know - he's been a voracious user and raiser of capital across public and private markets, and across sectors. If he made one point during his fireside address, it is that he's long energy in Australia and with the data centre build-out under way, that's a good place to be.
summary: The Macquarie Australia Conference highlighted a surge in interest from generalist investors in BHP and other companies pivoting towards AI and electrification, amidst rising capital costs and a focus on cost control.

heading: Data Science and AI
key_points: BHP's share price has risen due to new interest from generalist investors focusing on copper and AI trends.
Companies are adapting their business models to capitalise on the AI boom, with significant investments in data centres and technology.
Rising capital costs and inflation are prompting businesses to strengthen their balance sheets and control costs.
relevance: Understanding these trends is crucial for actuaries and financial services professionals as they navigate investment strategies and risk management in a changing economic landscape.
